canonical_examples.analysis: Socrates first elicits Euthyphro's proposed definition: the pious is what is dear to the gods. He then tests the definition against Euthyphro's own commitments that gods disagree, that the same act may be loved by some gods and hated by others, and that piety cannot be both pious and impious in the same respect. When Euthyphro revises the account to what all gods love, Socrates asks whether the pious is loved because it is pious or pious because it is loved. The elenchus exposes that divine approval is not yet an explanatory definition of piety; it either tracks an independent standard or makes piety arbitrary.
canonical_examples.problem: In Plato's Euthyphro, Euthyphro claims to know what piety is because prosecuting his father is pious, and Socrates asks him to define piety itself.

canonical_examples.analysis: Socrates elicits Meno's examples of virtue for men, women, children, and slaves, then rejects them as a swarm of instances rather than the one form by which all virtues are virtues. When Meno defines virtue as ruling over people, Socrates tests it with cases where rule is unjust or unavailable, forcing the addition of justice and moderation. That addition makes the definition circular, because justice is itself supposed to be a virtue. The elenchus shifts the inquiry from confident possession of examples to aporia about the common nature of virtue.
canonical_examples.problem: In Plato's Meno, Meno offers several confident answers to the question 'What is virtue?'

core_claims: A person who cannot give a stable logos of a virtue or concept does not yet possess the knowledge they claim, even if they can cite many examples.
Cross-examining a speaker's own accepted premises can refute a definition without importing an external doctrine.
Definitions of ethical concepts must state what the thing is in itself, not merely list instances, effects, approvals, or conventional associations.
Aporia is an epistemic achievement: recognizing contradiction and ignorance is better for inquiry than retaining a confident but unstable opinion.
Moral inquiry is inseparable from self-examination; the examiner tests not only propositions but the speaker's claim to live knowingly.
An adequate account must survive counterexample, circularity checks, and priority questions about whether the proposed criterion explains or merely follows the target concept.
description: Socratic Method | elenchus is definition-seeking cross-examination that elicits a person's commitments, tests them for contradiction, and uses the resulting aporia to clarify what is and is not yet known.
failure_modes: Eristic drift: the examiner starts trying to win or embarrass rather than to clarify, producing compliance, resentment, or verbal defeat without understanding.
False aporia: a contradiction may reflect ambiguous wording or an unfairly compressed premise, not a genuine failure in the interlocutor's view.
Overvaluing negative results: repeated refutation can destroy bad definitions without supplying constructive criteria for action.
Asymmetry of control: the questioner controls pace, terms, and admissible answers, which can make the exchange look rational while smuggling in assumptions.
Intellectualist bias: the method can imply that ethical competence requires explicit definitions, underestimating tacit skill, habituation, or tradition.

key_vocabulary.Aporia: A state of puzzlement or impasse reached when an interlocutor sees that their answers do not cohere. In elenchus it is not failure but the honest starting point for renewed inquiry.
key_vocabulary.Counterexample: A case that satisfies the proposed definition but should not, or fails the definition while still belonging to the target concept. Counterexamples show that the logos is too broad, too narrow, or misdirected.
key_vocabulary.Elenchus: Socratic refutation by question and answer, using the interlocutor's own admissions to reveal inconsistency. It aims to test a claim to knowledge rather than merely exchange opinions.
key_vocabulary.Form: The common nature or 'what-it-is' sought behind many instances. Socratic definition-seeking asks for this unity rather than a list of examples.
key_vocabulary.Logos: An account or reasoned definition that can be stated, defended, and tested. Possessing a logos is treated as a mark of knowledge rather than mere true belief.
key_vocabulary.Priority question: A question about whether the proposed standard explains the target or merely depends on it, as in whether the pious is loved because it is pious.
key_vocabulary.Refutation: The demonstration that a set of accepted claims cannot all be true together. In Socratic practice it targets the speaker's claim to know, not just a sentence in isolation.

name: Socratic Method
operative_procedure: State the target of inquiry as a 'What is X?' question, preferably about a contested virtue, value, or concept rather than a request for examples.
Elicit the interlocutor's initial logos and record it as a general definition, not as a slogan or single case.
Separate examples from essence: ask whether the answer names many X-things or the one feature by which all of them are X.
Draw out auxiliary commitments the interlocutor accepts, especially about opposites, sameness, excellence, justice, benefit, knowledge, or divine/human approval.

Test the definition against cases and implications that the interlocutor is likely to admit, looking for contradiction, circularity, excessive breadth, excessive narrowness, or failure of explanatory priority.
When inconsistency appears, restate the inconsistent set charitably and ask which premise the interlocutor is willing to revise.
Require a revised logos and repeat the test, tracking whether the new answer avoids the prior refutation or merely changes words.
End by naming the aporia: specify what has been learned negatively, what remains undefined, and which assumptions must be examined next.
scope_and_limits: Elenchus is strongest when a participant claims conceptual or moral knowledge and is willing to answer questions under rational pressure. It is less suited to empirical discovery, urgent action, trauma-sensitive dialogue, or contexts where the relevant competence is practical and tacit rather than definitional. Its product is usually clarified ignorance, a narrowed question, or a better definition, not a complete positive theory.
source_summary: Authored from model knowledge of Plato's Socratic dialogues and standard secondary scholarship on Socratic elenchus, without fetched source text.

source_texts.title: Gregory Vlastos, Socrates: Ironist and Moral Philosopher (1991)

when_to_use: Someone confidently uses a moral, political, or conceptual term but cannot say what makes cases fall under it.
A dispute persists because parties trade examples and labels without testing a shared definition.
A claim to expertise or certainty needs to be examined through the speaker's own commitments rather than by outside authority.
A proposed definition seems circular, too broad, too narrow, or dependent on approval rather than explanation.
The immediate goal is disciplined clarification or exposure of contradiction, not quick consensus or quantitative prediction.
